shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: from the meadow floor, straight up: blades framing a burning blue dome

segments.text: Every citizen of this garden lives under the same roof. The ant glimpses it between towers. The fish reads it through a trembling ceiling. The birds fly in its lowest room. None of them has ever wondered what it is.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the full dome, horizon to horizon, over the small complete world

segments.text: We will wonder for them.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: a slow rotation across the whole heaven

segments.text: The sky.

shot.subject: sun
shot.notes: dawn: light floods over the rim of the world, shadows racing across the field

segments.text: Its first duty is fire. When the sun clears the rim, the garden switches on — light races down the grass towers, the flowers open for business, and every cold-bodied flier on the meadow spreads itself to the heat.

shot.subject: sun
shot.notes: the sun's arc traced across the dome in accelerating light

segments.text: The lamp then crosses the roof on its faithful arc, and the whole world tells time by it. Shadows are the garden's only clocks: long, short, long — morning, noon, evening — the same sentence read aloud daily to residents who cannot read anything else.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: golden hour: light raking sideways through backlit blades

segments.text: And twice a day, at the arc's ends, the lamp becomes an artist. Low light turns the grass ocean to fire and the water to metal. If this series has a signature image, the sky painted it.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: clouds assembling: white fleets crossing the blue in formation

segments.text: Between dawn and dusk, the dome dresses and redresses. Clear mornings; fleets of cloud by noon; gray wool before rain. Each costume changes the light, and the light changes everything — colors, temperatures, tempers.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the lake swapping wardrobes in sync: silver, pewter, slate

segments.text: The mirror below copies every change, an hour of mood in every minute. Watch sky and lake together and the garden seems to be thinking — considering something enormous, in colors.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: gliding beneath a darkening cloud base as the last blue closes

segments.text: And when the dome goes truly dark at midday, every resident knows the grammar: the sky is about to speak in its other voice. That voice gets its own episode. For now — the roof holds.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: clean midday blue, the dome at its emptiest and deepest

segments.text: Spare an hour for the sky doing nothing, because its nothing is a masterwork. That noon blue is not a ceiling's paint; it is depth itself — sunlight scattered and rescattered until the whole dome glows with the leftovers. The residents stand at the bottom of an ocean of lit air, and not one of them ever looks up long enough to notice.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: a lone small cloud crossing the empty blue, its shadow crossing the field below

segments.text: Into that emptiness, a single cloud is an event. Watch one cross the dome trailing its shadow across the meadow like a slow hand — flowers dimming and reigniting in its wake, the air cooling a degree and recovering. One cloud, one shadow, one shiver through the grass economy. The sky cannot touch the garden gently without the garden answering.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the horizon band at midday: pale, hazed, where dome meets the world's rim

segments.text: At the rim, the blue always thins. The horizon wears a pale band at every hour — distance itself made visible, the thickest possible slice of air between any eye and the edge of the world. It is the one part of the roof that admits how the trick is done: the sky is not a thing above the garden. It is the garden's air, wearing light.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the light imperceptibly warming as the afternoon begins its long descent

segments.text: And all through the blue hours, the great gradient is already moving. Noon's white light ages into afternoon gold so slowly that no resident could name the moment of change. The sky's finest work is done at exactly the speed that cannot be watched — which may be the best definition of a day this series can offer.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: sunset in full production: the western dome in flame, the east already dimming

segments.text: Evening is the dome's great scene change. The lamp drops to the rim, the fire spreads, and then — with a patience no theater could afford — the light simply leaves.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the meadow surrendering color: flowers dimming, grass going gray-blue

segments.text: Below, the shift change we saw in episode two: day fliers landing, night lanterns lighting. The garden does not resist the dark. It has staff for it.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: rising into deepening blue as the first stars pierce through

segments.text: And the dark, it turns out, is not darkness at all. It is the dome's second act — the roof becoming a window, and beyond it, an audience of fixed lights taking their seats.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: full night: the dome crowded with stars over the black field

segments.text: By full night, the transformation is complete. The same sky that burned blue and gold now stands deep and pierced with silver — patterns among the stars that the next episode will name one by one.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon rising over the water, laying its cold lane on the lake

segments.text: The night lamp rises in its turn, gentler than the first, with its own strange power — for as we shall see, that pale disc does not merely light the water. It moves it.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: night: grass silhouettes against the star field, a firefly drifting through frame

segments.text: And under it all, the garden sleeps or works by kind — a world with one roof, two lamps, and no idea how well it is lit.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the star field fills the frame; certain stars seem to brighten, suggesting shapes

segments.text: Look longer at that window and the lights begin to organize. A hunter. A great bear. A swan flying down the dome. Someone has drawn pictures in the garden's night.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: a single constellation traced faintly against the dark

segments.text: Next: the Stars.
